CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow (Introduction → Datasets → Model → Experiments → Conclusion) and explicitly states its three main research questions early in the introduction. Notation is minimal and consistently used for the described model; the methods and experiments are described in a readable way. However the presentation has minor polish issues: several spelling/typo errors (e.g. "avalable", "suprisingly"), some duplicated/garbled text and a poorly formatted Table 4/appendix region in the provided extract, and occasional repetition which reduces readability. Overall the manuscript is clear and informative but would benefit from careful copyediting and cleanup of the figures/tables/appendix material. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow (Introduction -> Datasets -> Model -> Experiments -> Conclusion) and explicitly states its main contributions early via three numbered research questions in the Introduction. Notation is minimal and consistent and the AS Reader model is described clearly in Section 3. However presentation has minor issues: several typos and misspellings (e.g. "avalable", "suprisingly"), duplicated/repeated text and messy appendix/table fragments, and a few figure/table references that feel awkward or are not shown inline, which slightly hampers readability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow (Introduction → Datasets → Model → Experiments → Conclusion) and explicitly states its three main research questions early in the introduction. Notation is minimal and consistently used for the described model; the methods and experiments are described in a readable way. However the presentation has minor polish issues: several spelling/typo errors (e.g. "avalable", "suprisingly"), some duplicated/garbled text and a poorly formatted Table 4/appendix region in the provided extract, and occasional repetition which reduces readability. Overall the manuscript is clear and informative but would benefit from careful copyediting and cleanup of the figures/tables/appendix material.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow (Introduction -> Datasets -> Model -> Experiments -> Conclusion) and explicitly states its main contributions early via three numbered research questions in the Introduction. Notation is minimal and consistent and the AS Reader model is described clearly in Section 3. However presentation has minor issues: several typos and misspellings (e.g. "avalable", "suprisingly"), duplicated/repeated text and messy appendix/table fragments, and a few figure/table references that feel awkward or are not shown inline, which slightly hampers readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are empirically supported: Section 4.1 reports poor zero-shot transfer (pre-trained models evaluated on bAbI, see Table 4), Section 4.2 shows clear gains after target-domain adjustment (Figures 2/3 and Appendix D with p-values), and Section 4.3 demonstrates that both pre-trained embeddings and encoders contribute (Table 2 / Fig.2b). Experimental methodology is generally sound (multiple random splits, baselines, ensembles, and statistical tests) but there are minor caveats: the authors convert/anonymize bAbI to cloze form (Appendix A) which may change task distribution and some CNN/DM pretraining results are inconclusive per Table 7. Overall the experiments justify the conclusions, with only modest limitations in generality and comparability. | B(4): The paper's main claims (poor zero-shot transfer from BookTest/CNN-DM to bAbI/SQuAD, substantial benefit from small amounts of target-domain fine-tuning, and that both embeddings and encoder weights contribute) are directly supported by extensive experiments (3701 model instances), multiple random splits, reported means/stds and one-sided p-values (Appendix D). Experimental methodology includes reasonable baselines (random, most-frequent, and comparisons to MemN2N/DCR where available) and ablation-style tests (partially pre-trained embeddings vs encoders) that validate the claims. Minor concerns: conversion of bAbI to cloze-style and applying CNN/DM-style anonymization limits direct comparability across some conditions (authors note this), and the study is restricted to the AS Reader and single-word-answer subsets, which constrain generality but do not undermine the presented conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are empirically supported: Section 4.1 reports poor zero-shot transfer (pre-trained models evaluated on bAbI, see Table 4), Section 4.2 shows clear gains after target-domain adjustment (Figures 2/3 and Appendix D with p-values), and Section 4.3 demonstrates that both pre-trained embeddings and encoders contribute (Table 2 / Fig.2b). Experimental methodology is generally sound (multiple random splits, baselines, ensembles, and statistical tests) but there are minor caveats: the authors convert/anonymize bAbI to cloze form (Appendix A) which may change task distribution and some CNN/DM pretraining results are inconclusive per Table 7. Overall the experiments justify the conclusions, with only modest limitations in generality and comparability.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims (poor zero-shot transfer from BookTest/CNN-DM to bAbI/SQuAD, substantial benefit from small amounts of target-domain fine-tuning, and that both embeddings and encoder weights contribute) are directly supported by extensive experiments (3701 model instances), multiple random splits, reported means/stds and one-sided p-values (Appendix D). Experimental methodology includes reasonable baselines (random, most-frequent, and comparisons to MemN2N/DCR where available) and ablation-style tests (partially pre-trained embeddings vs encoders) that validate the claims. Minor concerns: conversion of bAbI to cloze-style and applying CNN/DM-style anonymization limits direct comparability across some conditions (authors note this), and the study is restricted to the AS Reader and single-word-answer subsets, which constrain generality but do not undermine the presented conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper does not propose a new model or algorithm but performs a systematic empirical study of transfer learning for reading comprehension: pre-training an AS Reader on large cloze datasets (BookTest, CNN/DM) and measuring transfer to bAbI and SQuAD under zero- and few-shot settings (Sections 4.1–4.3). It clearly differentiates from prior transfer work (which focused on classification/parsing) and reports novel findings (pre-training helps after small target adjustment and benefits both embeddings and encoders). Overall this is an informative, empirical contribution rather than a methodological advance, so its novelty is moderate (incremental but useful). | B(3): The paper does not introduce a new model or algorithm: it uses the existing AS Reader and standard pre-training/fine-tuning on large cloze datasets (BookTest, CNN/DM) and evaluates transfer to bAbI and SQuAD. Its primary novelty is empirical — claiming to be the first systematic study of transfer learning in reading comprehension and analyzing contribution of pre-trained embeddings vs encoders (Sections 4.2–4.3). Because the contribution is largely an experimental/analysis paper rather than a new method, the technical novelty is moderate (informative but incremental).

It clearly differentiates from prior transfer work (which focused on classification/parsing) and reports novel findings (pre-training helps after small target adjustment and benefits both embeddings and encoders). Overall this is an informative, empirical contribution rather than a methodological advance, so its novelty is moderate (incremental but useful).
